proposed_features.description: Some images show vessels containing visible liquid, while others are empty or contain solid objects only. This could separate functional containers from decorative or sculptural objects.
Some vessels contain recognizable food items (vegetables, broth, etc.), while others do not. This may distinguish between utilitarian and ornamental objects.
Some vessels have elaborate surface patterns or designs, while others are plain or have minimal decoration. This could indicate a difference in intended use or cultural context.
The material of the vessel (metal, ceramic, stone, etc.) is visually distinct and may correlate with the two categories.
Some images show the vessel in use (e.g., on a stove, with utensils), while others are displayed as objects (e.g., on a pedestal or shelf). This may separate functional from decorative items.
Some vessels have visible steam or are shown with heat sources, suggesting active cooking or serving, while others do not.
Some vessels have visible handles or lugs, which may relate to their function (e.g., for carrying or pouring).
Some vessels have simple, regular shapes (cylindrical, round), while others have complex or sculptural forms. This may indicate a difference in purpose.
The dominant colors of the vessels and their contents vary (e.g., earthy tones, metallic, blue/white patterns, food colors), which may be a distinguishing factor.
Some images include utensils (ladles, spoons) interacting with the vessel, while others do not. This may indicate active use versus display.
